description: Wednesday 28 January 2026 | Finance and Expenditure Committee The Treasury, Te Ara Mokopuna, 2025 Long-term Insights Briefing The Treasury, Long-term Fiscal Statement 2025 He Puna Hao Pātiki Investment Statement 2025 The Treasury Iain Rennie CNZM, Secretary to the Treasury and Chief Executive Chris Bunny, Deputy Secretary, Budget and Fiscal Performance Struan Little, Chief Strategist
descriptionSummary: The Treasury, Te Ara Mokopuna, 2025 Long-term Insights Briefing. The Treasury, Long-term Fiscal Statement 2025. He Puna Hao Pātiki Investment Statement 2025

chunks.text: Millions of the real budget operating allowances. We had some pretty high wins just prior to that, and then 2025 we're down to a billion. Now. We've just bumped up for the next three years, and going to be scared. So I guess what we heard from the Minister is that she's satisfied that we're going to get more accurate preview this time around. Can you explain to us how you're going to given that my leader said to me prior to the last election, I don't believe these numbers. It's way worse than it looks. It's just an old, wily guy that's got really good instincts. You didn't use

senior and of course, I believed you guys. So please tell me how we can believe you guys, and how it can affect us going into the next election. So it's confident. Look, I think, I think first, firstly, we take all of our forecast rounds really seriously. And, you know, I mean, I think, you know, we've got a solid platform about how we go about our forecast, but I'm wanting to make sure in my time as Secretary, we are clearly seen as as best practice globally, for the reasons that you say so. And Chris and I started talking about that and what that will look like, and that won't be completed by three foods, but my expectation is, each time we do a forecast in a methodological sense, better than last, and we'll present it in a stronger way, so we'll see that forecast stuff. Forecast look, I think, I think, having said all of that, you know, as I said a bit earlier, forecasting, particularly when you go out three, four years, you know, the the nature of economic science is, there's, there's a lot, lots of variability. And so I think we will keep painting that picture and painting the uncertainties. And then I think we move from sort of what I call forecasting, then to policy rights about how do others, any government, when you're faced with significant uncertainties over several years, develop their plans in a way that's going to be pretty robust. So those plans should just fall over if economies a bit weaker for a year or two, you know? So, so, you know, there are a number of ways we can address that, but I won't just be through our forecasts.

Yes, because, you know, the ballast, so to speak, where we've got some room as Chair said around being able to spend extra money in health is pretty tight, so we need to be aware of those questions. Yeah, so thank you for that. I have supplementary honourable Barbara Edmonds was talking about in terms of fiat currency, was your answer that there are some challenges there, and we haven't had a discussion in any of this around what's sort of looming in terms of blockchain and other currencies and exchange and finance, exchange, etc, we as a small trading nation, with, as you say, strong, strong institutions trusted. Are we going to attempt to edge that dynamic, because it is fast growing.

That's right. I mean, I think I was really struck. I travelled with Minister Edmunds, who went to the UK and talked with some of the regulators about, for example, the growing preference of the UK, case, younger males in particular, to hold their wealth and crypto, as opposed to real currency inside government, backed.

And I think, I think in the sense of that, that cracks issues for us around how we recognise the reality of of of our people are seeking to store value, and therefore, how does, how does policy respond in terms of transparency, regulation, etc, and so I think there's, there's quite a big agenda there. And some of this is actually driven by, for example, the US, current news, administration is pushing stable coin, yes, quite strongly, and it's something we will have to think a bit about. How do we, how do we respond around that? So, so this is an issue I think that's very live, certainly for Reserve Bank, for ourselves, for Fma, because I think NG. Like it or not. You know, we're not going to go back easily to a world where everyone's going to say, I'm just happy to store my wealth and dollars and cents, New Zealand dollars and cents. We seem to have kind of moved past that point. And so the issue is, how do we how do we acknowledge that world while keeping real strength around the institutions that we have around our fiat currency.

Are we blind to the to the transactions that are happening with crypto at the moment? So we were talking about spending in retail and that sort of thing a little while ago. Are we blind to that? Do we actually understand about trade is actually happening? Look, I think, I think I think I, I think the issue is, I think it is, it is hard to get a picture right. Yeah, I think we, it's pretty fair to say, probably in New Zealand, these issues are less present than they are in some other countries. But we can't be complacent.

Okay, it would be very nice to update you. Thinking, Okay, we've got a couple of subs here. We're going to go to, oh, she minds indeed. You get, if you're the stuff on this a very brief new primary. We've got no regulation around it at all. They're wanting to work with the Reserve Bank and other regulators to start actually, I guess, legitimising what the world is doing and trading and billions of dollars. So is there actually a work stream, a proactive work stream from Treasury in this space,

there's, there's, there's a work stream. It's what's called payments modernization, because we we effectively need to replace our current domestic payment system in the next few years, not not least because our F POS system has a limited life, and so in doing that, we need to think quite broadly. So at the moment, the Reserve Bank is leading a piece of work that will be reporting, in a strategy sense, to ministers in the first half of this year, and we're part of that, other agencies like, you know, Ng, a part of it. So there's a, there's a quite a strong whole of government focus. And I would imagine that, you know, post election, this, this will be, this will be a significant area of reform for whoever is government, because it's, it's it's needed. Okay? Todd Stevens

just quickly on haifu, I think Mr. Rennie, in your opening comments, you talked about since haife, just a bit of an update, and you said something like, the global outlook is a bit more stable or encouraging. And you mentioned particularly the US and AI. But just also want to get your reflection. Get your reflections on what we're seeing with China as well, because obviously that's another major trading partner for us. It seems things there might be slightly better than anticipated, but again, so China, you've got sort of, I'd say, short term, long term, so shorter term. Authorities in China have clearly are using fiscal policy more aggressively to support, particularly consumer spending. And it's that's a, it's a, it's part, small part of the wider global environments a bit stronger at the same time, China continues to have significant structural issues. So there's still a property overhang, a significant you know, population now is declining in China, so something like 3.4 million production in population last year alone. So some ageing population pressures are quite acute in China. So China remains very big. There's obviously group dynamism in parts of that economy at the same time, there are significant structural issues that are also impinging on the near term, so it's something we keep watching quite closely.

Okay? Now we got any more questions on Hanafi, Dr woods, no modes that long. Okay? Just particularly on haere, obviously, the minister made a number of kind of statements about fiscal prudence and otherwise, and her interpretation of what that means with regard to the price back to surplus, I was just wondering what advice if any, Treasury has been providing on the potential long term costs of some of the things that she also referenced, notably the cuts to funding for emergency housing, which officials have advised. Will increase the homeless population, and we're definitely seeing in the likes of mai electorate in Auckland Central, but also in the government's decisions to based on official advice, increase climate changing emissions. So just that broader point, I guess, when we're talking about the immediate government strategy and the budget policy statement and the kind of cost cutting exercises, then the potential long term costs of those short term decisions.

So the way I'd answer that is, look as a general one and truncate advice. You know, as you may recall, we have a living standards framework that that governs how we give advice, and that means that we think about issues, about sustainability, distributional issues, obviously financial and economic issues and so, you know, depending on the policy issue, you'll see the Treasury advice trying to both cover both short term issues, but also to draw out long term implications where that's relevant, yeah, I mean, just further to that point, I think we all obviously operate in this very political world in which there is kind of this reckoning, or attempt to kind of, you know, create a narrative about what is happening. And obviously that does have meaning with regard to the animal spirit of the economy and the views that people have about spending and otherwise. But how is the general public supposed to follow, you know, the likes of the living standards framework? How should they engage with understanding the likes of those long term implications of these immediate government decisions when it comes to budgets on a day to day basis?

Well, it's actually a big question. I think partly it's, I'd go back to we, we have a very transparent system. So pretty much, you know, all the advice that we tender to a government, as with any agency, overwhelmingly, that gets released, whether that's proactively or response to to requests. And that's actually a way in which people can actually see see the advice and considerations that went before a government, and I think, is the most important thing. And obviously people like yourselves, media essentially, can also pick up on that in thinking about both those short term and longer term issues.

Okay, thank you for that. Just to put a little bit of structure in it for our live international audience, Mr. Rennie, we'll just regroup. And I just again, want to welcome you as as Secretary of the Treasury and on the TV side here on your left is Chris Bundy, deputy secretary. And of course, we've got Strawn little as chief strategist at Treasury. We've now got a session, and we'll use up to an hour on this of three reports, three significant reports that were released August, September and November. The first being long term insights briefing, the second being long term fiscal statement, 2025 and the third being investment statement, 2025 talking to you earlier, you were quite happy to kind of blend these through, but perhaps the best way to get us started, if you can just give us say the highlights. You know, look at the state of this report. I know it's not easy, but the top the elevator pitch for both those, for three of those reports, and then we'll open for questions.

Thank you. Thank you, Mr. Chair and and coming to your point about in a time variable, we won't be able to get into a lot of the detail, but on that, I mean, I've got Minister Willis's agreement that if any of the parties here or the committee want a more deep dive around particular issues and modelling, she's very happy for that. Oh, very thank you to do that. So thank you. Members want to contact my office. We can, we can see that. Thank you for that.

I think the bigger picture, I'd say, if I, if I, if I step through the two bit long term insights briefing, a key take out of that is New Zealand, in the last 30 years has gone through a number of both minor and major shocks. When we look to the future, we see a world that is more shock prone. And a key message for us is that advisors and ministers need to build that expectation of shock into their fiscal fiscal planning, but also into some of the other policy frameworks we have as a as a country, and we have the rule of thumb based on the last 30 or 40 years, that cumulative impact of government response to shocks is around about 10% of GDP per decade.

Now we never, we never project that. So in thinking about Treasury forecasts, coming back to Dr Wilson's point, there is a system. Attic favourable bias, at least around our debt forecasts, and that's a bit of a segue into the long term fiscal statement and the investment statement, which looks at the fiscal position through through the lens of the balance sheet. So the key message I'd say around the long term fiscal statement is that the current set of public services that are either explicitly set out statute or implicitly are made to New Zealanders through current delivery, that mix of public services is going to be sustainable was going to require significant changes in either policy settings revenue over coming decades to be able to deliver that to New Zealanders. And I think the point we make is that the window to make those changes in a way that minimises disruption to the community remains, but the scope to do that is reducing. I think the third point we make around that is the likelihood is that no one policy lever can be pulled to make fiscal position sustainable. And reality is that governments over the next decade or so will need to work simultaneously on reducing some expenditure programmes, increasing revenues and thinking about how to optimise the balance sheet. The point I'd make on the investment statement is, firstly, the size of the Crown's balance sheet is is very large. It's something like $500 billion that is likely to increase over time, particularly as we address, I think, collectively, the decades of under investment that has gone on in aspects of our public infrastructure. So there are three, I think, key messages our investment statement, but deciding what to invest in is really important, and we see scope for governments to make better choices about what to invest in, how to do it, how much. And we don't think our system is as good as it can be in terms of the assets. And the reality is, the Crown will continue to own the vast bulk of assets that it owns. At the moment, we can do a much better job around managing that asset so that it delivers value, whether that's a commercial value or a social service value through time. And I think collectively, we have not managed our assets as well as we could have. And thirdly, I think there is a question, given the major investments that government will need to make over the coming decade or so, whether the government has completely the right mix of assets, and some of that is at an agency level, do people need continue to own a piece of land in a location given Where service delivery pressures are through to issues about whether the crown or the private sector should own part or of an asset. So I think those are the three big issues. So I leave it

there. Okay, we're gonna go to the honourable Dr Deborah Russell, this long term insights briefing is fascinating. I think it shows the value of the whole long term insights briefing process, and, I mean, we've had the debate about in the house yet, I'm really disappointed that not going to have them from individual departments in the future. But this one is great. So thank you for putting that together. Look, there's a lot we could dig into here, and I've read the general thing, if I'm going to take one message away from some of the fiscal tools analysis, it's that, you know, that sort of almost the one off subsidies, almost the best way to go, like the wage subsidy or the lump sum payment, and trying to put changes into the tax system because of the lags and things like that. It's just kind of hard. I guess, one of the things that I So, that'll be my key point. You'll have to tell me if you want me to take more out of that, other than that, in terms of the fiscal stuff, I guess in terms of responding to shocks, though. I mean, the thing that's got me is we've got a set of tools we can use now, fiscally, we need to do a lot more planning around adaptation and the sorts of stuff that's kind of hit us in the last few days and things like that. You've got a mention of climate change in here in terms of adaptation, but I'm just going to, I want to compare climate change in terms of adaptation. Team versus the ongoing response we need to climate change is the difference between the impact between a sudden earthquake, as we had with the Canterbury earthquakes, or the ones we know now we have as long slow breaks that go on for a long time. So I'm intrigued to understand whether you think of that ongoing response to climate change as being sort of something that could count as a shock, that's going to play out, play out over decades, rather than yes.

Okay, so if I answer your question two, Pati, so yes, I mean, I'm proud of a lot of work in this briefing, but one of them, I think it hopefully will be helpful to policymakers, is the assessment of the tools. And we've tried to draw on experiences that both the last couple of governments have dealt with both this one, and so I think it does, does give policymakers a sense about you know this, there's no necessary right or wrong, but I think you're, you're you're right. I mean, I think the sense about the very, you know, short term, timely targeted intervention, particularly around support for those businesses or communities or whatever can can be really important. You know, you can get sense at the other end of the spectrum. You know, shovel ready is good in theory. But I think as as successive governments, have to be honest, have found it's, it's more in theory than practice. And so I think, I think your your takeout, I think is probably nonsense margins, but I think it's broadly right. I think the your point about climate is right. And so if you think about our economy, we often very much think about the moment shocks like covid or a Canterbury Earthquake. But they can be both longer term shocks. So you know, for example, on the positive side, over the last 20 years or so, we've had a very significant increase in our terms of trade, and that is that has essentially made us a more commodity dependent economy, and it's put more pressures on other partner exporting, for example, manufacturing, etc, and you've seen this in Australia as well. So some of those shocks happen, and you're, you're right about, you know, some of the shocks connected with the energy transition, you know, we talked, I think, before Christmas, about the shifts in gas, for example, but over a longer period of time, for example, you know, the broader decarbonization, electrification will continue to play out. So I think you're absolutely right, Deborah, that shocks are both both the in the moment, but also these sort of decades long, you know, shocks and they're both relevant, and policymakers have to keep both of those in mind when they when they're setting their kind of broad policy programme.

Yeah, it's interesting, because the tools we've gotten here, the fiscal tools that we oriented, are very much oriented towards those short term, the instant shocks, as you say, and actually the useful tools, I'm glad we've tried them. Covid was quite an experiment and interesting to get the reflections out of it, to go, actually, this is probably better to go down this route than that route. But of course, we always covid go, Oh my goodness. Global pandemic. We don't know what was going to happen to the world. Whereas earthquakes are somewhat protected, we've mapped out the consequences of earthquakes quite well. Given our past experience. We know what to do. We didn't know what to do with covid and so but it was worth trying those things because now we know that actually, maybe this sort of thing doesn't work so well. That thing does. So it's just reflecting on the way we responded to those different sorts of crises. You know, because the crisis was different, because with the Canterbury Earthquake, I know it was a long and ongoing process with all the aftershocks, nevertheless, we know what's required in an earthquake. Didn't know what was required in a global pandemic, unless we sort of stepped back to 1918

Well, yes and no. I mean, I would, I would say, having my previous incarnation look, to be honest, there was a lot of making things up. Start with about Canterbury Earthquake responses, some of that worked and some that did not work. So part of for us as a treasury is trying to capture the experiences that we've had and say, What can we distil from them equally? I think you're right about no shockers in the future is going to be exactly the same as the shocks that we've had to date, but we want to learn as much as we can from those previous shots. So I guess I wouldn't

quite see a start bright line between we know how to deal with earthquakes. Because I don't, to be frank, I don't think we had really thought through what happens when our second major urban area gets disrupted, as opposed to a more localised shop. And clearly, you know, the pandemic stuff, you know, it wasn't actually that relevant going back to 1918 because New Zealand is a completely different society to that situation follows on. I was really interested. I think there's some fantastic work around there, around what those kind of fiscal tools we have to respond in looking at the various impacts, and I think in covid, we did actually reach to some of the things that had been developed in the earth. The wage subsidy scoring ban came directly from the earthquake recovery. I'm also interested in what work we need to do to prepare for you. Say there's a 10% impact on GDP every 10 years. Or was it 10% Yeah, 10% every 10 years. There's actually a stimulatory, potentially a signatory upside from some of these kind of shocks as well, when they involve rebuilds, and whether we've got the models right around how we maximise that for New Zealand, in terms of after Gabriel, we looked at some of the skirt, the skirt model, so the Alliance model, of contracting, for how we were going to do some of the rebuild, is there more we can do around having a toolbox of ensuring that we're ensuring the maximum spend in New Zealand post some of these shops, so that we are directly pumping back stimulus into into our own economy?

So I think, I think the short answer, what you're saying is, I think a there's more work we can do, yeah, and I think so, as interesting as you can imagine, there number of discussions around the most recent weather events. And I've been actually quite heartened to hear from some of the agencies that they're going back to Gabrielle and both trying to pick up what worked, but also things we can improve. So I think because Gabrielle is relatively it's reasonably fresh in people's minds that we're doing that, but I think there is more we can do so that actually we're as well prepared as you could ever be to meet those kinds of shocks. So

one of the things that has always struck me that it's Groundhog Day when it comes to the labour side of how we do a recovery piece, particularly when we have a regional event, which extreme weather events increasingly are going to be, not only how do we get labour there, but how we house them, and what we do around worker accommodation, and how we do that, is there any work going on of what a something bad has happened. Here's a lift and shift model of what we can do around a, getting workers there and B, making sure they can be accommodated, because that's often one of the biggest struggles.

Look, I think I'd say, Do we have a lift and shift model not? I don't think we have. I look, I think, I think you have to be kind of, you know, these, there's a certain degree that you can go to. So obviously, if you think a bit about the current weather event, you know, doing a lift and shift of getting labour into a Tara Fati, for example, they're going to be different issues that, if you had a major flood event, say, we store club. You know, it's not going to be one size. So I think there's a degree on which we, we can, we can probably do a bit better. I mean, I think the system is working relatively well to try and get pragmatic solutions for the realities that we're dealing with,

but I think it comes to that the investment that we make into anticipating the next the next shock. So actually, I think you just see that our briefing, from a treasury perspective, is that's what we're trying to do. But also that needs to be replicated, both in other agencies, which are more delivery focused, but also, how do we bring it together?

Which is, which is more of I think it's an issue for our colleagues in dpmc, and I think that that is one of the key questions, is that we always think, what is the governance the government, government, governance arrangements look like? How does it interface with local communities? We kind of have these conversations over and over, and we need to make sure that they are specific to the area. But is there more we could do? And given that there are potentially huge cost savings if we get this right from the get go, is this something the Treasury is looking at

like, I'd say we're not actually actively looking at this right at the moment? Yeah. You know, I think this is part of a conversation on the go base with tricky dpmc, because that's actually where the fundamental, the whole government governance, comes together. You know, together with the role at Nima, Treasury often has quite a, quite a, quite a say in it, given the level of expenditure. Yeah, absolutely.

Yeah, and but your point, I think, is right, because, from a treasury perspective, the the reality is that we have a kind of fit for purpose, embedded model. I think that is going to deliver better value for governments in terms of outcomes for communities, and it's probably going to be more fiscally better than if each time we have a bespoke, complex set of arrangements. And also reality is that you know that if the better we are about responding quickly to community needs, it's likely to be cheaper than actually if governments feel more under pressure, and therefore responses to have more expensive kind of interventions, whereas if we can get in at the front end and meet those community needs when they arise, I think that's likely to be a better option, because we often start

with kind of ad hoc payments rather than actually a systemic kind of response, and how quickly we could get that sup from Barbara Edmonds, thank you. Just related to responding through events out of government controlled like natural disasters, pandemics, etc, the investment statement made the statement that most of the borrowing that happened post 2019, so 100 point 1 billion was because, mostly because of the funding responses to the covid 19 pandemic, the Auckland anniversary weekend, floods in cyclone Gabriel. What is the total number debt bigger for those three events? There's around 60 billion for covid, isn't

it the well, the overall covid response package was 61 billion, but I don't have a number for you in terms of what, what the sum of those is, and I'm not sure they'd have an exact split of what it is, but you work it out backwards, because the covid itself was 20% of GDP and some of those other big disasters were Canterbury was about 11% other near we has about 1.7% so that's where we would have got those numbers from in terms and so you stand by I mean specific drivers of fire Debt, Including funding response to those three major events. So that's quite clearly, I mean, it doesn't have numbers here, but that's Treasury stands by that statement.

And, yeah, because, because those are very, very substantial sums and one off. And I think that's the point that sits behind the, you know, some of the work we've done, which is that the fiscal consequences of some of these events are very significant, and through time, you know, they can end up driving your overall fiscal position. And I think that's what's been happening here. And so you see that there's a chart and the long term insights briefing, that sort of Ratchet effect, and that's what's been going on. It's,

yeah, if you're you know, obviously the other thing is, in terms of the government's overall fiscal position and structural deficit, that's also been driving the debt levels. But in terms of the ratchet effect, it's these one off shocks that have really driven things in the last couple of decades. Thanks. Trent, okay. Dr David, just switch tacks a little bit here, well, quite a bit here, to focus in on superannuation. Really enjoyed your report. Thank you very much for that. I think it's well worth doing. It's just some idea about some of the factors that we're having to deal with, to address a major thing, such as the cost of super. We're looking here at once in part of time, the working age population went out weighed us by seven to one. I'm at the tail end of the tail end of the boomers. So I'm coming through soon. It looked more like one to four, and then it may be two to one. In the not too distant future, we have New Zealand super fit it with credits due. Thank you, Mr. Cullen, fantastic. $89 billion returns from that are not going to keep up. Are they anywhere near In fact, we haven't been able to draw down as much as we would like to subsidise superannuation. We are heading in the right direction, but, you know, we're not going to touch the golden egg. So there's only so much we can subsidise there. Migration, I noticed, has delayed it somewhat, because we've got a younger age coming into the country. That's an interesting dynamic. These are all sort of mitigating factors, working longer people are working longer. From Dan, I think it was no it was our friend whose dad has worked till 72 and that seems to be more and more the norm. But we also under can undervalue volunteerism, how that supports the economy. I know in Australia, they've valued this to see what it's actually worth to the economy. We haven't done such a thing here in New Zealand. The other mitigating factor is work in. Considering whether we extend the time or the age at which you're entitled to or whether or not it's more needs based. And another factor is Kei we save at the rise and Kei we save currently overtaken our super fund. It's around $29 billion and we can expect that to do more heavy lifting as we go forward. So you can see where I'm going with this question, because I think there's a real fairness problem with extending the age. Maori, by and large, are the worst effect, followed by Pasifika, in terms of the extension of the age, they get less benefit from superannuation and a dignified retirement, very important to us. So can you give us some sense of what you think the way forward is in terms of how we manage this bubble? You could, for example, say that we're going to address the bubble through the super fund. It's an interesting dynamic. Or we could say we're going to rely more heavily on KiwiSaver. So can you give us some sense of what you're thinking in terms of how we manage this? Because I see that you're going to say 6% of GDP before long, and that is

a huge thing. So that's a really big question, really good question. So the first thing I'd say, and this is certainly what we what's in our statement, and what we advise the government, is, look, I think, in thinking about the way forward, I think you need to think quite broadly about policy settings. You have to think about the role of KiwiSaver. Then to think about NZs, you have to think about the role of the super funds. So it's like a three legged stool, as opposed to just housing, biggest asset or whole Well,

that's right. And I think, I think the other, the other bit, without making this complicated, is you want to also want to think a bit about how some of the changes within the system impact on other parts of the welfare system. How does it impact on aged care, policy, settings, accommodations, etc, etc. So you look broadly, I think if we went back in time, I mean, New Zealand has a very strange retirement income system compared to most countries. It is, it is most countries have put more weight on what I call the Save as you go system, which of which? In our context, I'd say, Kei, you saver, and potentially other other forms of private savings relative to public. And I think it's, it's, it's likely through time, you probably want to think about strengthening the Save as you go pillar. And you know, success of governments have increased the contributions to KiwiSaver through time. But I think there is behind this. And coming back to your point about the age of fairness, fairness also needs to be looked at and around so you know, acknowledge what you say, and at the same time, if you're moving towards a Save As you go system, what is the fairness in expecting younger generations to essentially have their real wages reduced through higher KiwiSaver contributions, and At the same time experience higher tax rates for generations, whether they're in retirement or closer to retirement. Is that fair? Now, ultimately, the you know that's your job in terms of deciding what is an intergenerationally fair way to distribute those costs and benefits for us as a treasury. One of the things I really like in long term fiscal statement is our modelling now now allows us, in a way we didn't have before, to make some of those implications a bit more explicit to ministers, and that's something our our role. But I think you know what? Why is that? Why is this hard? In a policy sense, I leave politics to one side. In a policy sense, it is a bit about, how do you distribute that the costs of adjustment across different generations? Because reality is that, unfortunately, there is no free lunch here, and so you need to think about fairness, both in the specific but also the different how fairness plays out over different cohorts and different respects. Which is, which is why I think we, we sort of say, look at it in the round, and actually why governments, I think, at least, need to be. Open to potentially working on a broad range of tools, you know, so we haven't just said there's one lever. And it's also why we make a very strong pitch about the importance of gradual change. We don't need to, as a country, solve this problem for the next 40 years, because there's what, 13 elections between now and 40 years time, but actually at least over the next, you know, 1015, years. How do you do you make the poll, the policy settings look more secure and sustainable.

Final question, do you think there should be a roof taken to an independent remuneration authority where it gets takes the politics out of it, and it's done pragmatically, phased in, over time, and it just depoliticizes it, and based on demographics, numbers, facts? Mr. Banks darling, because it's intriguing. I think governments have an array of choices, absolutely so all of the options are largely known. So we wouldn't tell you anything that you wouldn't already know in terms of what levers you can pull, but when you if you chose to continue super settings as they are currently constructed, but offset the spend through another saving in government that's wider than what a remuneration could look at.

So I think, I think there's two levels. So I think ultimately you can't take politics out of this, because there is a trade off. I mean, because there'll be potentially some players that just say, look, I. We're cool with the current settings. We just raise taxes, and we're cool about having the argument. There'll be other people who have a different position. And you've just seen that recently in Denmark, for example, where I think pushed up to 72 so that could be an example about about how you you know, if there's a big agreement on the big levers, you can kind of, in one sense, depoliticise some of the, what I call the sort of implementation pathway, but I just don't think you can ultimately take the politics out of the big choices.

and on. So we talked about superannuation. You've said, let's, you know, more that we can move to a, you know, save as you go. Model is preferred. Let's talk about health care. Okay, thank you. Okay, any other questions? Dr Megan Woodson, then we'll go to Chloe. Thank you. One of the things I think, in the long term, forecast the geopolitical races, I mean, almost impossible, probably, to kind of assess at the moment they seem to be coming from everywhere. Are you building scenario models, and how is this going to be reflected into the into the projections as we go forward.

So I think, I think there's a number of elements there. I mean, I think you're, you're right about what I call, broadly, the economic security lens of treasury is becoming, and will become much more important. And some of that is in the scenario space and as of support decisions that governments make about, you know, some of it's about external events about it. For example, if the Strax or moves were closed, what does that do around oil prices and therefore the economy? But then, if New Zealand chooses to take some foreign policy actions, what might be some of the domestic policy implications, if there is some coercive or retaliatory impact, we have to think a bit about how we can support, you know, decision making there, so at least ministers have some sense about. Potential domestic economic implications of stances that are taken. So I think there's that element. I mean, I think our board economic surveillance role has also is also strengthening. We're looking, you know, constantly, about what's happening in both markets policy. Obviously, we're working with our colleagues, particularly in fat more closely around those issues. I think more generally, I think there's, there's an issue about in in the context of a contested geopolitics, what, what are the steps a New Zealand government can take to make New Zealand economy more resilient, and so that's everything from thinking about supply chain issues for both the public sector, but also, how does the private sector think about that, and how does government make the private sector aware of those issues so that firms can make, you know, good, good decisions through to issues. For example, I talked a little bit about financial infrastructure, so and the need for us to make significant changes to our financial infrastructure next few years. How do we do that in a way that tries to ensure that New Zealand retains, you know, sovereignty and is less open to coercion compared to various other arrangements. So, so it's a, really, our role is, it's what says multifaceted, you know? And I'd say, you know, it's, it's an emerging role. So we try to work pretty quickly, but it's a, it's quite a shift for us as an organisation.

So do you have a large team working? Look, I think it permeates, you know, a lot of areas of our business, you know, right from, you know, we've had to think about, as you know that the next set of Ferris are coming from the Guangzhou shipyard. Yeah. How do we, you know, how we think about that, how we support ferry holdings, about that, through to the parts of our modelling capacities, through to our policy advice we give on financial so I think it's less a single team, but it's more one that it's actually going through across a number of areas of treasuries work, and we're trying to integrate that as best we can.

I'm really interested scenarios that we're now having to think about that even 12 months ago would have seemed fencible, like the fact that Europe and China could drop all its US bonds as a retaliatory measure. For example, how do we kind of keep, I mean, things are moving so fast in directions that we never anticipated. What are we doing to try and keep our analysis in line with it?

So I think, I think there's several. One is the work that we do directly. There's the agencies we work with in that in our public sector system, there's our counterparts, because, I mean, I've had conversations with my UK counterpart, my Australian counterpart, we're all grappling with the same issue, so we're trying to share how we're all responding, both in an intelligence sense, but also a function sense. And also we're trying to tap into more some of our big relationships with the big multilateral organisations, like some of the International Monetary Fund, because they're thinking about a lot of these issues as well. So we're trying to do it both, both within outside and outside New Zealand.

Yeah, the long term insights briefing primarily, kind of frames school policy as an insurance against shocks. So ultimately, you know, there's, there's, there's a couple of levels to that, you know, one, one is as a there's a government's kind of a broad strategy. But what's the trajectory about public finances we want to be on and within that you can make different choices versus, do you want to go to the fence being of 2% of GDP, or do you want to, for example, invest in aggressive kind of climate related spending? Those are sort of micro decisions that play out in each budget. They actually give latitude to any government to. Make those their tough choices. I think the other, the other piece that we have is in thinking about, for example, the advice that we give around things like debt, etc, is recognising a lot of this is around, how do governments think about risk? How do you and you're saying which I agree with Chloe's, yeah, there's, there's, yeah, there's no, unfortunately, no, riskless judgement that governments are making now or will be making the future. And it's those, those those trade offs, I think what we bring to the table is a perspective as a finance ministry. So, you know, obviously we are, you know, we bring to the table the things that you'd expect us to bring to the table. I think, having said that, I'd say, you know, you can't say that we've been we've underforecast public debt. Our record is to the to the contrary, and I think that shapes a little bit about me. I think reality is we're likely to have quite a bit more depth in the future than we forecast, than the other way around. But ultimately, these are the choices that governments are going to have to take and balance,

I guess, just further to that point, how would you respond to critiques from certain economists or otherwise with regard to the economic orthodoxy and how Treasury tends to, as you've just alluded, to, have a very good handle on the cost of things, but not necessarily on the benefits, particularly the long term benefits, and also the ability for certain types of spending to prevent or mitigate certain types of disasters or further costs.

I think, I think, look, if I think about the critique, there's a there's a degree about people come to a different view about risk. And that's, yeah, that's fine, you know, no, I say, I'd say, I will defend the approach that we're taking about risk and knowledge or other other judgments. I'd be less accepting the point that we weight costs and don't think about different kinds of of benefits. You know, I think we've through the living standards framework, some of the tools that we've taken we try and think about various kinds of benefits that can come from different kinds of government spending. Obviously, we're in a world quite often where some of those benefits can be a little bit more abstract and less evidence based. So you always expect treasury to be sceptical, rather than less sceptical, end of that. But I think we try, as best we can, to kind of give a balanced set of views to ministers about the trade offs that they face.

Could you elaborate on the point about the methodological approach that Treasury uses to, for example, look at kind of preventative spending. Look, I think we certainly and kind of trying to do that, I think, and, you know, the minister talked when she was here about, you know, kind of investor save, and we certainly as an organisation, are trying to be very positive about working with agencies around that. So I would think that we are trying to capture some of the benefits from that preventative end. I think at the same time, you know, we also have to recognise the limitation. So if you took take an example, for example, you can argue, well, actually, let's increase the farming funding to farm at some various kinds of drugs, and that's going to take pressure off the health

system, healthcare system, in a kind of intellectual sense, you kind of say yes, but the reality is, because of how the healthcare system is very hard to go and so actually, I'm going to, you know, take out 20 or 30 surgeons or nurses, because, you know, so and so he's got a drug that means that we may have less of a surgical intervention, so that can be harder to operationalize, and so I think that will be reflected in the advice.

yeah, I guess what I'm trying to get to here is, you know, there's what we're trying to do with this committee, is provide public accountability and transparency and totally tackle more. And if it is, if I'm to kind of read between the lines, there a bit more of an ad hoc, discretionary approach that people at Treasury take for each of these areas, or if there is a specific methodology that you are currently applying to each of these instances,

commenting from the budget, we're not generally the initiators on a lot of these ideas, so the role that we play, in that sense, is to give them a good level of scrutiny to make sure That decision makers are actually short of the costs and the benefits, and that can be over quite a period of time. The Minister earlier referred to Innes kind of investment proposal and their systems, so that was a good example of where we looked at that as a bespoke investment, because I don't think there would be a framework that would apply to that, as well as, say, health spend to be assured that the benefits that were promised would actually accrue. Some of the work that we do initiate that we have a little bit more discretion on Ian's reference, the economic security work for investment in supply chain resilience, that would be one where you know the benefits, they're not easy to quantify, but it's a piece of work that we know would hold New Zealand better stead than the absence of it. the point that I'm kind of, is that laying there those assumptions then means that there's real clarity about, you know, how we kind of came to these conclusions. Okay, thank you. New Zealand has good careers, good credit ratings, lower dip other countries, but the forecasts indicate that that's not always going to be the case. I mean, I think, I think the general point I'd make is in thinking about how do we what we're trying to do is fund the Crown's debt obligations, both existing and additional, at least cost within The risk parameters that the government sets. And so that's the that's the overarching piece. And obviously we think a bit about what the right mix of instruments, what's the mix in terms of issuance across the yield curves, the balance between, you know, if you like standard bonds, and, for example, inflation linked security. Yield curve, you know, at the moment in Japan, obviously, but, but obviously, we've seen, seen developments along the end of the yield curve in places like France and UK and even the US. So we're kind of mindful of that, and take that into account in terms of our decisions, accepting, though, that, you know, the point you made at the start, I think is really important that at the moment, New Zealand, by the standards of the very big countries, has has lowered significantly lower levels of debt, and there is high interest in participation in our bond market. And it comes back to the conversation we had earlier about, I think there's real value in New Zealand government maintaining those fundamentals so that actually we can tap into those global markets. Because, you know, we're routinely needing, you know, 60 to 70% of our bonds are purchased by foreigners. So we are, we are heavily dependent on on fiscal deficits and existing debt being refinanced by foreigners, and so we need to give confidence to people that they are happy to participate in our market and purchase our debt at rates that we think are appropriate. Thank you.

Okay. Nancy Lin, can I just have us up on that? I'm keen to understand your analysis and comparison of New Zealand not to the large economy and the large population markets, but to the smaller economy and smaller population, because one of our advantage is being small and flexible and agile and you know, and we can move around in the Very risky markets. But what is our comparison to the smaller market that you analyse in Treasury?

So, and I think we use this as a graph, I think in long term insights briefing. So you know, if you look at product debt levels internationally, there's a small number of very large economies have a lot of debt. So US, Japan, UK, France, and debt levels are much, much higher than they are in New Zealand. You then look at the small, medium sized economies, and broadly, New Zealand has gone from having a debt that was quite a lot lower spend those economies to broadly about the same level. So we're sort of what it says average for a small, medium sized economy. And you know, coming back to what's happened last few years, you know, I mean, most of those economies have broadly similar levels of debt as they had going into covid That's slightly higher. We've obviously got much high levels of debt a few years down the track. So there's a bit of a distinction about our trajectory compared to some of our what we call our peers, as small European economies. So that's how we would see it,

and a follow up on that. But my interpretation of how our debt have increased so dramatically in a relatively short period of time, like just in the few years around covid period that is unsustainable. We can't, cannot see, well, I cannot see New Zealand going further into debt in that level of speed and size, and so in your analysis, comparing to the smaller and medium economy, what is your perspective on our debt level in particularly in that rise the true trip, I mean, going forward, obviously, you know, the government has a fiscal plan in place so that debt kind of broadly stabilises And it comes down. So what I think you sort of see by the end of the forecast period is that our debt levels are certainly sitting a little bit below what's expected in our peer economies. And obviously we've done that. We do that with a relatively significant reduction in our structural deficit over the next three or four years. So that's the trajectory that current government's policy intentions are moving towards.

Mr. Rennie, you also say you make clear on page 74 just on debt per GDP, that maintaining net debt below 50% of GDP would maintain sufficient fiscal space to enable governments to respond to future shocks. So you still strongly believe in that?

Yeah, broad. I think, I think what was trying to say there is, you know, we can keep looking at the appropriate level of prudent debt. And it is, it's context specific, but the key issue is less that the number. But actually, you know, you're what, what Flex is there for a government to respond to a shock, but then get back down on the other side, and when you look at that, you make some assumptions, you get around that 50% of GDP, so, but it's more the thinking behind the. The idea, rather than the number that I get to hang up.

Okay, members, we're just about getting to the end of this. Dan do the vote of thanks. Dan's only just getting started, just going back to that so, so the 50% that's the government's position, which is good. I'm wondering whether Treasury has done any work on you know, at what point does the debt level and the servicing costs just become unsustainable and you go into a spiral? And I know there was research 10 years ago from Rogoff and Reinhardt around the 100% in large economies, but that's not necessary fit for New Zealand. So you know what level is basically the point of no return for New Zealand in

terms of debt. Yeah. So we did some work back in 22 looking at what prudent debt level is, and the way we did that was use the IMF methodology, which does exactly that. It's hardly it's not an exact science, but that number was around 90% of GDP, which meant we could stabilise making some assumptions about interest rates and the growth rate. Now we deliberately chose some conservative assumptions so those are contestable. But the thinking behind that is that if you get into that uncomfortable position of high debt, it is very likely that your interest rates will be rising, and so therefore you do need to be conservative, but we will need to go back and look at this again as well. But that's why we ended up with 50% we said, Okay, if, if the sustainable area is about 90% how much provision do we need to take the shocks? And goes back to member lose point that the small open economies are much more shock prone, and so we need to make more of an allowance for those shocks than some of the bigger economies.

look, I think, I think I'd say it's, it's really important, because you can, you can think about building buffers at a couple of ways, obviously at the big picture, overall levels of debt.
